questions.prompt: What special feature is Lesson 12 first looking for in the Routh table?
options: a pair of poles on the jω axis
a zero in the numerator only
questions.correctAnswer: a pair of poles on the jω axis
questions.explanation: The first part of the lesson studies how a special Routh-table pattern reveals poles exactly on the imaginary axis, not just general left-half-plane or right-half-plane cases.

questions.prompt: In Example 3, what placeholder does the note use when a zero appears in the table?
questions.explanation: The note replaces the zero by a small epsilon so the recursion can continue and the sign behavior of the first column can still be examined carefully.

questions.prompt: Which Routh-table row is singled out in the note for detecting one pair of poles on the jω axis?
questions.explanation: Page 30 states the condition in terms of the entry indexed by s^1. That specific zero, together with the rest of the first column being positive, indicates one pair on the jω axis.

questions.prompt: If all first-column entries are positive but the one indexed by s^1 is zero, the note says there is:
options: one pair of poles on the jω axis
one pole in the right half plane
questions.correctAnswer: one pair of poles on the jω axis
questions.explanation: That is the specific rule given in the lecture note. It marks a boundary case rather than an ordinary stable or unstable classification.

questions.prompt: Why is a zero in the Routh table important in this lesson?
options: because it can signal a boundary case that needs special interpretation
because it means the numerator is zero
because it always proves the system is stable
because it eliminates the need for the rest of the table
questions.correctAnswer: because it can signal a boundary case that needs special interpretation
questions.explanation: The note uses the zero to expose a special situation near the stability boundary. It is not treated as a routine arithmetic inconvenience.

questions.prompt: In the note's Example 4, what type of pole pair is identified?
questions.explanation: The note concludes that the pattern corresponds to poles on the jω axis, which means a purely imaginary conjugate pair rather than real poles.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of checking the first column in the Routh array?
options: to decide stability behavior and detect special boundary cases
questions.correctAnswer: to decide stability behavior and detect special boundary cases
questions.explanation: The first column is the decisive part of the Routh table. In this lesson it is used not only for stability signs, but also for recognizing poles on the imaginary-axis boundary.

questions.prompt: What pair does the note obtain for the worked jω-axis example?
questions.explanation: The note explicitly evaluates the special pair and writes it as plus or minus j times root 2, which places both poles on the imaginary axis.

questions.prompt: Why should you not immediately label the system unstable when a zero appears in the Routh table?
options: because the zero may instead indicate poles on the jω axis
because zero entries are always ignored
because the numerator decides stability next
because the sign changes no longer matter
questions.correctAnswer: because the zero may instead indicate poles on the jω axis
questions.explanation: This lesson is built around exactly that caution. A zero in the table can reveal a boundary case, so you must interpret it using the specific rule from the note.

questions.prompt: What is the safest exam habit from this concept?
options: build the table carefully, inspect the first column, and interpret any zero entry explicitly
skip the table and guess from coefficient signs
solve the polynomial roots first and use Routh later
ignore the row labels and focus only on the final row
questions.correctAnswer: build the table carefully, inspect the first column, and interpret any zero entry explicitly
questions.explanation: That is the most reliable way to avoid losing marks. The lesson's whole point is that the special zero case carries meaning, so it must be handled and explained clearly.
